infobox.value: is a [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area") that can be created from any land owned or controlled by the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") by [proclamation](/wiki/Presidential_proclamation_(United_States) "Presidential proclamation (United States)") of the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") or an act of Congress. National monuments protect a wide variety of natural and historic resources, including sites of geologic, marine, archaeological, and cultural importance.[[1]](#cite_note-npca-1) The [Antiquities Act](/wiki/Antiquities_Act "Antiquities Act") of 1906 gives presidents the power to proclaim national monuments by executive action. In contrast, [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national parks of the United States") in the U.S. must be created by Congressional legislation.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Some national monuments were first created by presidential action and later designated as national parks by congressional approval.[[1]](#cite_note-npca-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Monuments assigned protected status by presidents of the US

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Devils_Tower_as_Seen_From_the_Path_Along_the_Base.jpg/250px-Devils_Tower_as_Seen_From_the_Path_Along_the_Base.jpg)](/wiki/File:Devils_Tower_as_Seen_From_the_Path_Along_the_Base.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Navajo_National_Monument_Tsegi_Canyon_Betatakin_Dwelling_28-09-2012_9-38-03.jpg/250px-Navajo_National_Monument_Tsegi_Canyon_Betatakin_Dwelling_28-09-2012_9-38-03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navajo_National_Monument_Tsegi_Canyon_Betatakin_Dwelling_28-09-2012_9-38-03.jpg)

[Navajo National Monument](/wiki/Navajo_National_Monument "Navajo National Monument"), Arizona

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Liberty01.jpg/250px-Liberty01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberty01.jpg)

[Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument"), New Jersey and New York

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Ft_Matanzas_2008.JPG/250px-Ft_Matanzas_2008.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ft_Matanzas_2008.JPG)

[Fort Matanzas National Monument](/wiki/Fort_Matanzas_National_Monument "Fort Matanzas National Monument"), Florida

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), a **national monument** is a [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area") that can be created from any land owned or controlled by the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") by [proclamation](/wiki/Presidential_proclamation_(United_States) "Presidential proclamation (United States)") of the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") or an act of Congress. National monuments protect a wide variety of natural and historic resources, including sites of geologic, marine, archaeological, and cultural importance.[[1]](#cite_note-npca-1) The [Antiquities Act](/wiki/Antiquities_Act "Antiquities Act") of 1906 gives presidents the power to proclaim national monuments by executive action. In contrast, [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national parks of the United States") in the U.S. must be created by Congressional legislation.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Some national monuments were first created by presidential action and later designated as national parks by congressional approval.[[1]](#cite_note-npca-1)

[The 138 national monuments](/wiki/List_of_national_monuments_of_the_United_States "List of national monuments of the United States") are managed by several federal agencies: the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), [United States Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service"), [United States Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "United States Fish and Wildlife Service"), the [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management"), and the [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") (in the case of marine national monuments). Historically, some national monuments were managed by the [War Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") used the Antiquities Act to declare [Devils Tower](/wiki/Devils_Tower "Devils Tower") in [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") as the first U.S. national monument.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_monument_(United_States)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Boss_Pinkley_1934.jpg/250px-Boss_Pinkley_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boss_Pinkley_1934.jpg)

Supt. Frank "Boss" Pinkley – the southwestern national monuments, 1934

The Antiquities Act authorized permits for legitimate [archaeological](/wiki/Archeology "Archeology") investigations and penalties for taking or destroying antiquities without permission. Additionally, it authorized the president to proclaim "historic landmarks, historic and prehistoric structures, and other objects of historic or scientific interest" on federal lands as national monuments, "the limits of which in all cases shall be confined to the smallest area compatible with the proper care and management of the objects to be protected."[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Presidents have used the Antiquities Act's proclamation authority not only to create new national monuments but to enlarge existing ones. For example, Franklin D. Roosevelt significantly enlarged [Dinosaur National Monument](/wiki/Dinosaur_National_Monument "Dinosaur National Monument") in 1938. [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") added [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") to [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument") in 1965, and Jimmy Carter made major additions to [Glacier Bay](/wiki/Glacier_Bay_National_Park_and_Preserve "Glacier Bay National Park and Preserve") and Katmai National Monuments in 1978.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Early 20th century origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_monument_(United_States)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early 20th century origin")]

The [Antiquities Act](/wiki/Antiquities_Act "Antiquities Act") of 1906 resulted from concerns about protecting mostly prehistoric [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") [ruins](/wiki/Ruin "Ruin") and artifacts (collectively termed "antiquities") on federal lands in the American West.

The reference in the act to "objects of ... scientific interest" enabled President Theodore Roosevelt to make a natural [geological](/wiki/Geology "Geology") feature, [Devils Tower](/wiki/Devils_Tower "Devils Tower") in [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), the first national monument three months later.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Among the next three monuments he proclaimed in 1906 was [Petrified Forest](/wiki/Petrified_Forest_National_Park "Petrified Forest National Park") in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), another natural feature. In 1908, Roosevelt used the act to proclaim more than 800,000 acres (3,200 km2) of the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") as a national monument.

In response to Roosevelt's declaration of the Grand Canyon monument, a putative mining claimant sued in federal court, claiming that Roosevelt had overstepped the Antiquities Act authority by protecting an entire canyon. In 1920, the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") ruled unanimously that the Grand Canyon was indeed "an object of historic or scientific interest" and could be protected by proclamation, setting a precedent for the use of the Antiquities Act to preserve large areas.[[7]](#cite_note-Cameron-7) Federal courts have since rejected every challenge to the president's use of Antiquities Act preservation authority, ruling that the law gives the president exclusive discretion over the determination of the size and nature of the objects protected.

### Mid-20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_monument_(United_States)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mid-20th century")]

In 1918, President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") proclaimed [Katmai National Monument](/wiki/Katmai_National_Park_and_Preserve "Katmai National Park and Preserve") in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), comprising more than 1,000,000 acres (4,000 km2). Katmai was later enlarged to nearly 2,800,000 acres (11,000 km2) by subsequent Antiquities Act proclamations and for many years was the largest national park system unit.

[Petrified Forest](/wiki/Petrified_Forest_National_Park "Petrified Forest National Park"), [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), and [Great Sand Dunes](/wiki/Great_Sand_Dunes_National_Park_and_Preserve "Great Sand Dunes National Park and Preserve"), among several other [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national parks of the United States"), were also originally proclaimed as national monuments and later designated national parks by Congress.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Substantial opposition did not materialize until 1943, when President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Delano_Roosevelt "Franklin Delano Roosevelt") proclaimed [Jackson Hole National Monument](/wiki/Jackson_Hole_National_Monument "Jackson Hole National Monument") in [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"). He did this to accept a donation of lands acquired by [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr."), for addition to [Grand Teton National Park](/wiki/Grand_Teton_National_Park "Grand Teton National Park") after Congress had declined to authorize this park expansion. Roosevelt's proclamation unleashed a storm of criticism about use of the Antiquities Act to circumvent Congress. A bill abolishing Jackson Hole National Monument passed Congress but was vetoed by Roosevelt, and Congressional and court challenges to the proclamation authority were mounted. In 1950, Congress finally incorporated most of the monument into Grand Teton National Park, but the act doing so barred further use of the proclamation authority in Wyoming except for areas of 5,000 acres or less.

### Late 20th century and early 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_monument_(United_States)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Late 20th century and early 21st century")]

The most substantial use of the proclamation authority came in 1978, when President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") proclaimed 17 new national monuments in Alaska after Congress had adjourned without passing a major Alaska lands bill. Congress passed [a revised version of the bill in 1980](/wiki/Alaska_National_Interest_Lands_Conservation_Act "Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act") incorporating most of these national monuments into national parks and [preserves](/wiki/National_preserve "National preserve"), but the act also curtailed further use of the proclamation authority in Alaska.

Carter's 1978 proclamations included [Misty Fjords](/wiki/Misty_Fjords_National_Monument "Misty Fjords National Monument") and [Admiralty Island National Monuments](/wiki/Admiralty_Island_National_Monument "Admiralty Island National Monument") in the U.S. Forest Service and [Becharof](/wiki/Becharof_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Becharof National Wildlife Refuge") and [Yukon Flats National Monuments](/wiki/Yukon_Flats_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Yukon Flats National Wildlife Refuge") in the [Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "United States Fish and Wildlife Service"), the first to be created outside of the National Park Service. The latter two became national wildlife refuges in 1980.

The proclamation authority was not used again anywhere until 1996, when President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") proclaimed the [Grand Staircase–Escalante National Monument](/wiki/Grand_Staircase%E2%80%93Escalante_National_Monument "Grand Staircase–Escalante National Monument") in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), after many years of unsuccessful advocacy by conservationists to protect parts of the area. This was the first national monument managed by the [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management"). This action was unpopular in Utah,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and bills were introduced to further restrict the president's authority,[[12]](#cite_note-12) none of which have been enacted. Most of the 16 national monuments created by President Clinton are managed not by the National Park Service, but by the Bureau of Land Management as part of the [National Landscape Conservation System](/wiki/National_Landscape_Conservation_System "National Landscape Conservation System").

President George W. Bush created four marine national monuments in the Pacific Ocean, the largest in the system: [Papahānaumokuākea](/wiki/Papah%C4%81naumoku%C4%81kea_Marine_National_Monument "Papahānaumokuākea Marine National Monument"), [Pacific Islands Heritag](/wiki/Pacific_Islands_Heritage_Marine_National_Monument "Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument"), [Mariana Trench](/wiki/Mariana_Trench_Marine_National_Monument "Mariana Trench Marine National Monument"), and the [Rose Atoll Marine National Monuments](/wiki/Rose_Atoll_Marine_National_Monument "Rose Atoll Marine National Monument").[[13]](#cite_note-13) They are managed by the Fish and Wildlife Service, with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration overseeing the fisheries. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") significantly expanded two of them and added a fifth in the Atlantic Ocean, the [Northeast Canyons and Seamounts Marine National Monument](/wiki/Northeast_Canyons_and_Seamounts_Marine_National_Monument "Northeast Canyons and Seamounts Marine National Monument").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

On June 24, 2016, Obama designated the [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") and surrounding areas in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), New York as the [Stonewall National Monument](/wiki/Stonewall_National_Monument "Stonewall National Monument"), the first national monument commemorating [the movement](/wiki/LGBT_movements_in_the_United_States "LGBT movements in the United States") for [LGBT rights in the United States](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Obama's establishments included several others recognizing civil rights history, including the [César E. Chávez](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_E._Ch%C3%A1vez_National_Monument "César E. Chávez National Monument"), [Belmont–Paul Women's Equality](/wiki/Belmont%E2%80%93Paul_Women%27s_Equality_National_Monument "Belmont–Paul Women's Equality National Monument"), [Freedom Riders](/wiki/Freedom_Riders_National_Monument "Freedom Riders National Monument"), and [Birmingham Civil Rights National Monuments](/wiki/Birmingham_Civil_Rights_National_Monument "Birmingham Civil Rights National Monument").

In December 2017, President Donald Trump substantially reduced the sizes of [Bears Ears](/wiki/Bears_Ears_National_Monument "Bears Ears National Monument") and [Grand Staircase–Escalante National Monuments](/wiki/Grand_Staircase%E2%80%93Escalante_National_Monument "Grand Staircase–Escalante National Monument"), removing protections on about 2.8 million acres of land where mining could resume.[[16]](#cite_note-Schneider-16)[[17]](#cite_note-WaPo_uranium-17) Three lawsuits challenged the legality of this action in federal court,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and in October 2021, President Joe Biden reversed the changes.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The restoration of the monuments has been challenged in court in an attempt to attack the Antiquities Act.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

President Biden's proclamations establishing and expanding monuments often incorporated consultation with Native American tribes for management and planning.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2025, Trump's interior secretary Doug Burgum ordered a review of all withdrawn public lands including national monuments for their exploitation for drilling and mining.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
